title: Dog Vaginal Prolapse: Causes, Urgency, and Triage Advice
meta_desc: Learn what causes vaginal tissue prolapse in dogs, why it is a high-priority emergency, and how photos help your veterinarian with triage.
content_html:
What is Vaginal Prolapse?

Vaginal prolapse occurs when the vaginal lining becomes excessively swollen and protrudes through the vulva. It typically appears as a pink or red, doughnut-shaped mass of tissue. This condition is most common in young, large-breed female dogs during the first few heat cycles when estrogen levels are high, causing the vaginal tissues to enlarge and displace.

Is it an Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. While a vaginal prolapse might look like a simple swelling, it is a veterinary emergency. The exposed tissue is extremely delicate and can quickly become dry, cracked, or infected. In severe cases, the tissue can become necrotic (die) or obstruct the dog's ability to urinate. Immediate professional care is required to protect the tissue and prevent long-term complications.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of the protruding tissue can be incredibly helpful for your veterinary team. A visual allows the vet to assess the color and moisture level of the tissue before you even arrive. It also helps them differentiate between a vaginal prolapse and more severe conditions like a uterine prolapse or a tumor, ensuring they are fully prepared for your dog's specific needs upon arrival.

faqs.question: Should I try to push the tissue back in myself?
faqs.author: No. Never attempt to push the tissue back inside, as you could cause severe trauma, pain, or infection. This must only be performed by a veterinarian.

faqs.question: What can I do for my dog on the way to the vet?
faqs.author: Keep the area moist using a water-based lubricant (like K-Y Jelly) or a clean, damp cloth. Use an Elizabethan collar to prevent your dog from licking or biting at the tissue.

faqs.question: Will my dog need surgery?
faqs.author: Treatment depends on the severity. Some cases resolve with hormone management and lubrication, but others may require surgical repair or an emergency spay to prevent recurrence.
